question: A company is running an HPC (High Performance Computing) simulation that requires the lowest possible network latency between 8 EC2 instances and needs them to communicate at 10 Gbps. Which placement group type should they use?

options.D: No placement group — use Enhanced Networking instead

explanation: Cluster Placement Groups pack instances close together within a single Availability Zone, enabling ultra-low latency and high-bandwidth (10 Gbps+) networking between instances — ideal for HPC and tightly-coupled parallel workloads. Spread groups maximise fault tolerance by separating instances. Partition groups are for distributed systems like Hadoop/Kafka. Enhanced Networking helps but is not a substitute for placement group locality.

question: A company runs a critical application across 6 EC2 instances. They must ensure that a single hardware failure cannot take down more than one instance at a time. Which placement group type meets this requirement?

options.A: Cluster Placement Group — instances are packed tightly for resilience
options.B: Spread Placement Group — each instance is on distinct underlying hardware
options.C: Partition Placement Group — instances share hardware within a partition
options.D: No placement group — AWS distributes instances automatically

explanation: Spread Placement Groups place each instance on separate physical hardware (separate rack, power, and network), so a hardware failure can only affect one instance at a time. This is the highest isolation for small critical workloads. Cluster groups are the opposite — they co-locate instances. Partition groups reduce blast radius per partition but instances within a partition can share hardware. AWS auto-placement does not guarantee this level of isolation.

question: Which placement group type is most suitable for a large distributed database like Apache Cassandra with 48 nodes, where you want to limit the impact of hardware failures to a subset of nodes?
explanation: Partition Placement Groups divide instances into logical partitions (up to 7 per AZ), where each partition has its own set of hardware. A failure in one partition does not affect others — perfect for distributed systems (Cassandra, Kafka, HDFS) that replicate data across nodes. Cluster groups offer no fault isolation. Spread groups are limited to 7 instances per AZ — not enough for 48 nodes. 'Regional Placement Group' is not an AWS service.

question: What happens to an EC2 instance's RAM contents when you Stop the instance?

options.A: RAM contents are preserved on the EBS root volume and restored on next start
options.B: RAM contents are lost — the instance starts fresh on next boot
options.C: RAM contents are stored in S3 and restored automatically
options.D: RAM contents are preserved in Instance Store until the instance restarts

explanation: Stopping an EC2 instance powers it down completely — RAM (memory) contents are lost. On restart, the OS boots fresh from the EBS root volume. This is why Stop is used for maintenance or cost-saving when state doesn't need to persist in memory. Hibernate is the feature that preserves RAM to disk.

question: A developer has an EC2 instance running a complex in-memory calculation that takes 4 hours to reach its current state. They need to stop the instance overnight and resume exactly where they left off in the morning. Which EC2 feature should they use?

options.A: Stop the instance — RAM is preserved automatically
options.B: Create an AMI snapshot before stopping
options.C: Hibernate the instance — RAM state is saved to the EBS root volume
options.D: Terminate the instance and restore from the last EBS snapshot

explanation: Hibernate saves the entire RAM contents to the encrypted EBS root volume before powering off. On restart, the OS resumes from exactly where it left off — processes, open files, and in-memory state are all restored. Regular Stop loses RAM. Creating an AMI captures the disk state but not the in-memory calculation state. Terminate is permanent.

question: Which of the following is a prerequisite for enabling Hibernate on an EC2 instance?

options.A: The instance must use Instance Store as the root volume
options.B: The EBS root volume must be encrypted and large enough to hold RAM contents
options.C: The instance must be in a Cluster Placement Group
options.D: Hibernate requires an HPC instance family (such as hpc6a)

explanation: Hibernate requires: (1) an encrypted EBS root volume — AWS enforces this because RAM may contain sensitive data; (2) the root volume must have enough free space to store the full RAM contents. Instance Store cannot be used (it's ephemeral). Hibernate works with most general-purpose instance types, not just HPC families. Placement groups are unrelated to Hibernate.

question: What is the key difference between Stopping and Terminating an EC2 instance?

options.A: Stop keeps the EBS volumes; Terminate permanently deletes the instance and (by default) its EBS root volume
options.B: Stop is only available for instances in private subnets
options.C: Terminate preserves the EBS root volume; Stop deletes it
options.D: There is no difference — both actions produce the same result

explanation: Stop shuts down the instance but preserves the EBS root volume — you can restart it later. Terminate permanently destroys the instance and, by default, deletes the EBS root volume (though this can be changed via the 'DeleteOnTermination' setting). Stop/Start also changes the public IP (unless an Elastic IP is used). Terminate is irreversible.

question: A Cluster Placement Group's main trade-off compared to a Spread Placement Group is:

options.A: Higher cost per instance in exchange for lower latency
options.B: Maximum performance but all instances are in one AZ — a single AZ failure loses all instances
options.C: Instances can span multiple Regions for global low-latency access
options.D: Lower latency within partitions but higher latency between partitions

explanation: Cluster Placement Groups deliver the highest network performance but place all instances within a single Availability Zone. If that AZ has an outage, all instances in the group are affected simultaneously — so there is zero fault tolerance. This is the key trade-off: maximum speed vs. zero HA. Spread groups sacrifice some performance for isolation across AZs. Placement groups don't span Regions.
